Name: Sea Lettuce
Location: Clean tidal creeks, oyster beds, private leases
Compliance: Check water-quality closures, shellfish areas, private property, and harvest rules
Notes: High-yield gateway seaweed for salads tempura powders and ferments; safest path is through approved private leases or aquaculture partners.

Name: Gracilaria
Location: Clean estuarine areas and aquaculture-adjacent sites
Compliance: Check water-quality closures, shellfish areas, private property, and harvest rules
Notes: Useful for broths gels and fresh crunch; confirm species identification and legal harvest location.

Name: Wakame
Compliance: Confirm species and site permissions; avoid contaminated marina water
Notes: Cold-season dock and rope seaweed lead; better treated as a research/acquisition target than casual public harvest.

Name: Sea Grapes
Compliance: Avoid wild collection without expert ID and permissions
Notes: Best acquired through tank culture or specialty growers; high chef value as a briny garnish.

Name: Glasswort / Sea Beans
Location: Salt marsh edges and private marsh access
Compliance: Confirm public-land rules, private property, and marsh harvest restrictions
Notes: One of the strongest Lowcountry forage crops for fresh crunch pickles and sauteing.

Name: Sea Purslane
Location: Salt marsh edges and sandy coastal margins
Compliance: Confirm access and avoid protected dunes or sensitive habitat
Notes: Succulent saline green; harvest lightly and only where access is legal.

Name: Marsh Elder
Compliance: Confirm access; use positive ID
Notes: Aromatic marsh plant with potential for teas seasoning and coastal pantry experiments.

Name: Muscadine Grapes
Location: Fence lines, woodland edges, private land with permission
Compliance: Get permission on private land; avoid roadside contamination
Notes: Abundant late-summer fruit for eating sauces ferments and preserves.

Name: Chickweed
Compliance: Avoid sprayed areas; verify ID
Notes: Mild winter-spring green for salads pesto and soups.

Name: Plantain
Compliance: Avoid sprayed areas and roadsides
Notes: Common edible green with practical culinary and herbal uses.

Name: Wild Onion / Garlic
Compliance: Must smell like onion or garlic; avoid lookalikes
Notes: Scallion-style wild green for late winter and spring cooking.

Name: Smilax Tips
Compliance: Harvest tender new growth only
Notes: Asparagus-like spring shoots; high return and easy to recognize once learned.

Name: Pokeweed
Compliance: Young leaves only and must be properly boiled; toxic if mishandled
Notes: Traditional Southern green but not beginner-friendly because mature plant parts and improper prep are dangerous.

Name: Lamb's Quarters
Compliance: Avoid sprayed areas; confirm ID
Notes: Useful cooked green similar to spinach.

Name: Amaranth
Compliance: Avoid sprayed areas; confirm ID
Notes: Summer green and seed crop; good heat-season forage.

Name: Wood Sorrel
Compliance: Use moderately due to oxalic acid
Notes: Bright lemony garnish and salad accent.

Name: Chanterelles
Location: Oak and pine forests after rain
Compliance: Never eat wild mushrooms without expert confirmation
Notes: High-value summer mushroom; ID discipline matters.

Name: Chicken of the Woods
Compliance: Never eat wild mushrooms without expert confirmation; cook thoroughly
Notes: Meaty mushroom useful as a seasonal protein-like ingredient.

Name: Oyster Mushrooms
Compliance: Never eat wild mushrooms without expert confirmation
Notes: Cool-season mushroom that can be common after wet weather.

Name: Beautyberries
Compliance: Get permission; best cooked or processed
Notes: Useful for jellies syrups and ferments.

Name: Prickly Pear
Compliance: Handle spines and glochids carefully; confirm access
Notes: Edible fruit and pads with coastal-sandy habitat fit.
